text: A basic off-grid solar system has four components: solar panels, a charge controller, batteries, and an inverter. Solar panels convert sunlight to DC electricity. The charge controller regulates voltage to prevent battery overcharge. Batteries store energy for use when the sun isn't shining. The inverter converts DC battery power to AC for household appliances. Size each component based on your daily energy consumption.

To calculate your solar panel needs, first determine daily energy usage in watt-hours. List every appliance, its wattage, and hours of daily use. Example: refrigerator (150W x 8h = 1200Wh) + lights (60W x 5h = 300Wh) + phone charging (10W x 2h = 20Wh) = 1520Wh/day. Divide by peak sun hours for your location (typically 4-6 hours) and multiply by 1.25 for system losses. For 1520Wh with 5 sun hours: 1520 / 5 * 1.25 = 380W of solar panels needed.

Battery bank sizing for off-grid systems: take your daily watt-hour consumption, divide by battery voltage (typically 12V or 24V), then multiply by days of autonomy desired (typically 2-3 days without sun), and divide by depth of discharge (50% for lead-acid, 80% for lithium). Example: 1520Wh/day ÷ 12V × 3 days ÷ 0.5 = 760Ah of 12V lead-acid battery capacity needed. Lithium batteries cost more upfront but last 3-5x longer and can discharge deeper.

Solar panel placement is critical for maximum output. In the Northern Hemisphere, face panels true south (not magnetic south). Tilt angle should equal your latitude for year-round production, or latitude minus 15° for summer optimization, or latitude plus 15° for winter optimization. Avoid shade — even partial shade on one cell can reduce the output of an entire panel by 50% or more due to the series connection of cells.

MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) charge controllers are 15-30% more efficient than PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) controllers. MPPT controllers convert excess panel voltage into additional charging current, which is especially beneficial in cold weather when panel voltage increases. For systems larger than 200W, MPPT is strongly recommended despite the higher cost. Size the controller for your total panel wattage plus 25% headroom.
